glossary.def: The set of all possible outcomes of an experiment, written Ω. An event is any subset of it, and a probability assigns each event a number in [0, 1].
The three rules all probabilities obey: non-negativity, total probability one, and additivity over mutually exclusive events. Every theorem in probability follows from them.
The probability of A given that B has occurred, P(A|B) = P(A∩B)/P(B). It renormalizes belief to the smaller world where B is known to hold, and is the engine of all learning from evidence.

Two events are independent when knowing one tells you nothing about the other, equivalently when their joint probability factors: P(A∩B) = P(A)P(B). It is a property of the probabilities, not of the physical situation.
The rule that inverts a conditional: P(H|E) = P(E|H)P(H)/P(E). It updates a prior belief into a posterior by scaling it with how well the hypothesis predicts the evidence, then renormalizing.

The error of judging P(H|E) from the likelihood P(E|H) while ignoring the prior P(H). It is why a 99%-accurate test for a 1%-prevalence disease is correct only about one in six times it fires positive.
Confusing P(evidence|innocent) with P(innocent|evidence). A one-in-a-million random-match probability is not a one-in-a-million chance of innocence; the two differ by the prior-over-evidence factor.

As independent samples accumulate, their running average converges to the true expectation E[X]. The convergence is slow, with error shrinking like 1/√n, and it links Kolmogorov's abstract probability to long-run frequency.

refs.cite: Kolmogorov, A. N. (1933). Foundations of the Theory of Probability.
Bayes, T. & Price, R. (1763). An Essay towards solving a Problem in the Doctrine of Chances. Phil. Trans. R. Soc.
Blitzstein, J. K. & Hwang, J. (2019). Introduction to Probability (2nd ed.). Chapman & Hall / CRC. (Harvard Stat 110)
Tversky, A. & Kahneman, D. (1983). Extensional versus intuitive reasoning: The conjunction fallacy in probability judgment. Psychological Review 90(4).
Jaynes, E. T. (2003). Probability Theory: The Logic of Science. Cambridge University Press.
